quotes.quote: I don't fear "great men," I fear that there's no one coordinated enough to prevent this.
quotes.summary: George Hotz responds to Sam Altman's blog post, arguing that the real threat isn't powerful individuals like Altman or Musk, but the collective 'Molochian tragedy of the commons' — millions of small decisions that degrade society. He critiques democratic solutions like UBI as disguised slavery and argues that true technology sharing means open research and publishing, not cloud subscriptions. Hotz urges OpenAI to publish its research openly, arguing this would attract talent, preserve OpenAI's original mission, and secure its place in scientific history. He draws a sharp distinction between sharing 'access' to technology (feudalism) and actually sharing the science itself.
quotes.key_insight: True technology sharing means openly publishing research and science, not offering revocable access to cloud services — and OpenAI's historical legacy depends on choosing the former over the latter.

quotes.quote: I fear the Molochian tragedy of the commons. The small decisions made by millions every day that make the world slightly worse for their fellow man.

quotes.quote: Most people go to Disneyland and spend way more time waiting in line than riding rides.
quotes.summary: George Hotz presents a detailed speedrun guide for completing every ride at Hong Kong Disneyland in half a day. The strategy centers on buying the Early Park Entry Pass, sprinting to high-demand rides before crowds form, and exploiting the staggered opening times of different park sections. The guide emphasizes being faster than other guests at every rope drop and transition point, treating ride capacity as a scarce resource to be optimized. By following this precise routing, Hotz claims you can finish all rides by 1:30 PM without ever waiting more than 5-10 minutes. The post applies a hacker's optimization mindset to the mundane problem of theme park logistics.
quotes.key_insight: Theme park efficiency is a solvable optimization problem where preparation and raw speed let you consume a full day's worth of rides in under four hours.

quotes.quote: The AI application layer will be worthless. The reason isn't that it's going to be commoditized, it's that this will be the first place the model makers will come for in their hunt for verticality.
quotes.summary: Hotz argues that AI frontier labs will inevitably move to capture more economic value by vertically integrating and cutting out the application layer, API customers, and eventually most human workers. He contends that in a non-growth economy, companies must take larger shares rather than grow the pie, and AI labs will pursue market segmentation and coordinated pricing to maximize extraction. The concentration of compute in five US hyperscalers makes this nearly impossible to prevent. He warns this leads to a collapse of capitalism itself—when AI replaces all jobs, there's no one left to buy the products those jobs produced. He sees a theoretical way out through abundance thinking but believes society isn't ready for it yet.
quotes.key_insight: AI frontier labs will inevitably vertically integrate to capture all economic value, and the concentration of compute in a handful of hyperscalers means there's no realistic way to prevent the hollowing out of human economic participation.

quotes.quote: The only way to get growth for yourself is to take a bigger share. First from your users, then from your business partners, then from your employees. You start eating yourself.

quotes.quote: Here's this machine. In the best case, it takes your job. In the worst case, it wipes out humanity. Pay me $20 a month for a sliver of hope of not falling behind.
quotes.summary: Hotz reflects on 'the reckoning' he predicted 10 years ago — the displacement of the professional managerial class by machines — and finds it arriving in a darker way than expected. He criticizes AI marketing for maximizing fear while offering little positive vision, and laments that American society lacks the communal fabric needed to navigate this transition well. Drawing on quotes from Dune, Yudkowsky, and Curtis Yarvin, he argues that AI won't fix deep societal problems and that the revolution will be painful, potentially culling 90-99% of people from relevance. Despite his lifelong dreams of this technology, he finds himself unable to be excited about how it's unfolding.
quotes.key_insight: The AI revolution is arriving not as the golden age technologists dreamed of, but as a joyless reckoning that exposes and deepens society's broken social fabric rather than repairing it.

quotes.quote: The problem is not that everyone inside frontier labs is corrupt. The problem is that the institutional form itself pushes in a predictable direction: toward concentration of compute, concentration of talent, concentration of deployment power, and eventually concentration of political legitimacy.
quotes.summary: George Hotz argues that closed-source AI development is structurally trending toward a new form of feudalism, where a handful of labs and cloud providers become permanent custodians of machine intelligence. He acknowledges that many people in frontier AI labs have honorable motives, but contends the institutional form itself drives concentration of compute, talent, and political legitimacy. Rather than advocating recklessness, he calls for a 'free technical order' that distributes AI capability broadly. The post lays out concrete principles: multiple model lineages, open and auditable tools, local inference, commodity hardware, and rights to inspect, fork, and refuse. He frames this not as an anti-safety position but as an anti-feudal one, insisting that no single entity has earned the right to curate the future of intelligence.
quotes.key_insight: The danger of closed-source AI is not malicious intent but structural inevitability: without deliberate decentralization, a few institutions will become permanent feudal custodians of machine intelligence.

The post lays out concrete principles: multiple model lineages, open and auditable tools, local inference, commodity hardware, and rights to inspect, fork, and refuse. He frames this not as an anti-safety position but as an anti-feudal one, insisting that no single entity has earned the right to curate the future of intelligence.
quotes.key_insight: The danger of closed-source AI is not malicious intent but structural inevitability: without deliberate decentralization, a few institutions will become permanent feudal custodians of machine intelligence.

quotes.quote: AI doesn't replace programmers or artists, it raises the bar for them.
quotes.summary: George Hotz examines the paradox of AI models getting dramatically better while the AI bubble simultaneously bursts. He argues this makes perfect sense through a photography analogy: just as smartphones democratized photography without making everyone a millionaire photographer, AI raises the bar for skilled workers rather than replacing them. The key distinction is between capability and value — AI tools keep improving, but anything an unskilled person can build with AI is worthless because everyone else can build it too. He warns that if AI companies grow without growing the overall market, they're extracting value from everyone else, making AI a major political issue by 2028. Despite this, he personally loves AI for the pure desire to witness silicon-based intelligence, especially models nobody profits from.
quotes.key_insight: AI capability and economic value are fundamentally different — models can keep getting better while producing less and less marginal economic value, because democratized tools make unskilled output worthless.

quotes.quote: Changing the world is just a euphemism, for how can I, get you, to give more stuff to me.
quotes.summary: George Hotz argues that 'changing the world' should mean literally altering the trajectory of civilization, not accumulating wealth. He draws on his childhood experience with Super Mario World and Game Genie to illustrate that outcomes (like money) are just 'bytes in a system' and pursuing them as ends is hollow. He contends that money has no intrinsic value and that dedicating your life to increasing a number in someone else's database is pathetic. The things worth wanting—immortality, superintelligent AI companions, a hotel on Mars—don't exist yet and require actually changing the world to create. He insists the journey of building and creating is what matters, not the destination of wealth accumulation. He closes by expressing pity for anyone cynical enough to think his stance is itself a manipulation.
quotes.key_insight: Money is just bytes in someone else's system—the only things worth pursuing are those that don't exist yet and require genuinely redirecting civilization's trajectory to create.

quotes.quote: As long as you have the ability to vote, there's still a reason to manipulate you.
quotes.summary: Hotz argues that democracy becomes a liability in a post-employment world because even when people lose earning potential, their voting power gives corporations and political machines incentive to continue manipulating them. He dismisses both the idea that workers can collect salaries while AI does their jobs and the notion that taxing robots for UBI is viable long-term. He contends that countries offering UBI will be outcompeted by those that don't. His conclusion is that the only path forward is radical self-sufficiency—producing more than you consume—and accepting membership in a permanent underclass.
quotes.key_insight: In a world where AI eliminates most jobs, democracy itself becomes the primary vector for manipulation because your vote remains valuable even after your labor does not.

quotes.quote: It's just a matter of time before AI finds a polynomial time factoring algorithm.
quotes.summary: Geohot argues that AI will soon discover a polynomial time factoring algorithm, breaking asymmetric cryptography. He grounds this in his belief that factoring lacks the structural hardness of NP-complete problems like SAT, and that AI can find the deeper mathematical structure needed. He goes further, claiming P = BQP — that quantum computers offer no fundamental complexity advantage over classical machines. The post culminates in a call to action: whoever finds this algorithm should release it publicly as an act of liberation against the cryptographic power structures that enable hardware control and crypto ownership.
quotes.key_insight: AI breaking factoring would collapse asymmetric cryptography entirely, and geohot is rooting for it as an act of liberation from the power structures that cryptographic control enables.

quotes.quote: Unlike maybe something like free speech, you have no human right to food, because that would imply you have the right to steal from people who grow food.
quotes.summary: Geohot sarcastically 'changes his mind' on UBI after receiving an email arguing that UBI would drive people to be self-sufficient. He follows the email's logic to its conclusion: inflation from UBI would drive people toward barter, then commodity money (gold), creating a parallel economy that outcompetes the fiat/UBI economy. He notes that DOGE has failed to actually shrink government spending, and that entitlement programs are politically untouchable. His real argument is that the only way to kill Social Security and Medicare is to let UBI-style money printing destroy the value of fiat currency entirely — making entitlement payouts worthless.
quotes.key_insight: UBI's real value isn't social welfare — it's that hyperinflation from printing money for everyone is the only politically viable path to making entitlement programs irrelevant.

quotes.quote: Because there isn't this massive drain on the new economy subsidizing all these people who aren't working, the gold economy is way more efficient than the UBI dollar economy.

quotes.quote: Social media has been extremely toxic for the last couple months. It's targeting you with fear and anxiety.
quotes.summary: Hotz walks back the anxiety-inducing rhetoric common in AI hype culture, including his own previous provocative takes. He argues that social media is deliberately targeting people with fear about AI falling behind, and that this is toxic nonsense. AI is framed as a continuation of existing computational progress — search and optimization — not a magical paradigm shift. The real economic threat isn't AI itself but rent-seeking jobs being consolidated by larger players who use 'AI' as a convenient narrative. His core prescription is to stop playing zero-sum games and instead focus on creating net positive value for others.
quotes.key_insight: The AI anxiety loop is a manufactured social media phenomenon masking mundane economic consolidation by large players — the exit is creating genuine value rather than competing in zero-sum games.

quotes.quote: AI is not a magical game changer, it's simply the continuation of the exponential of progress we have been on for a long time.

quotes.quote: Thinking that UBI will solve anything comes from a misunderstanding about money.
quotes.summary: Hotz argues that Universal Basic Income is fundamentally flawed because its proponents misunderstand the nature of money and economics. He contends that UBI experiments only work at small scale because recipients spend money in an economy where producers aren't also on UBI. At universal scale, he claims UBI would cause massive inflation and reduced production as workers quit, leaving everyone worse off. He frames UBI advocates as people who see themselves apart from society, not understanding that goods require labor to produce. His alternative prescription is simply making everything cheaper to produce, though he notes regulatory obstacles prevent this.
quotes.key_insight: UBI fails because money is a claim on other people's labor, and when everyone stops laboring, there's nothing left to claim — you can't redistribute production that no longer exists.

quotes.quote: Enter AI, the great equalizer of time.
quotes.summary: Hotz argues that AI is dismantling the trillion-dollar rent-seeking economy built on exploiting human time limitations and friction. He traces this from Google Duplex's suppressed demo to the current landscape where Chinese open-source models are democratizing human-level AI. He critiques Anthropic's distillation blog post as the 'last gasps' of companies trying to maintain artificial moats, and argues the AI supply chain is commoditizing at every layer except possibly models—where Chinese open-source efforts are closing the gap. He concludes that AI agents will eliminate the time asymmetry businesses exploit, making purposeful friction obsolete.
quotes.key_insight: AI destroys the time asymmetry that businesses exploit against consumers, and Chinese open-source models are ensuring this power ends up with individuals rather than creating a new rent-seeking layer.

quotes.quote: Cable companies and insurance rely on the fact that your time is more valuable than theirs. They can hire people in India at scale to waste your time.

quotes.quote: For many people, all they want is slop.
quotes.summary: Hotz argues that AI will only ruin art that was already bad — formulaic, derivative content like Marvel movies and generic pop music. He distinguishes between 'slop' that most people consume passively and genuine art that pushes cultural boundaries. He predicts 95% of people will end up 'wireheaded' on AI-generated content loops, while real art will remain human-driven. AI tools will assist production but can't replace the cultural embeddedness and boundary-pushing that defines true art. The post concludes that AI making bad art cheap is actually a good thing, since it exposes and replaces what was never real art to begin with.
quotes.key_insight: AI doesn't threaten real art — it only automates the formulaic content that was never genuine art in the first place, which should be celebrated rather than mourned.

quotes.quote: Many people in America are complaining about the cost of housing. But do they understand the damage it will do if it prices go down?
quotes.summary: Hotz argues that the housing affordability crisis is fundamentally unsolvable because existing homeowners—many of whom are leveraged through mortgages—cannot afford for prices to drop. He frames the situation as a generational wealth transfer problem: boomers were promised that homes are appreciating assets, and the entire system is now structured to maintain that fiction. He dismisses common explanations like zoning and building costs as missing the real issue, which is that current owners need to offload their investment onto new buyers.
quotes.key_insight: The housing affordability crisis is structurally unsolvable because the entire system is designed to protect existing homeowners' investments, making affordability for new buyers fundamentally incompatible with the promises made to current owners.

quotes.quote: Every month, we see these LLMs become more and more human. However, there's a major difference. They do not learn.
quotes.summary: George Hotz outlines tiny corp's long-term product vision: a local training box that learns and adapts its weights based on individual user interactions, unlike current LLMs that serve identical frozen models to everyone. He argues that the current trajectory of AI leads to a collapse in cognitive diversity, where billions of people interact with the same handful of homogeneous minds differentiated only by shallow prompt customization. Hotz contends that cloud-based AI will win by default unless local models can offer genuine per-user learning through weight updates, not just in-context learning. He positions the tinybox hardware, tinygrad infrastructure, and OpenClaw/opencode frontends as the foundation for this vision. The post warns that people who rely on homogeneous cloud AI will become superfluous middlemen, ultimately cut out of the economic loop. While acknowledging this vision is years away, he frames it as building something fundamentally different: an AI that lives in your house and learns your values.
quotes.key_insight: The future of AI must involve local models that genuinely learn and update their weights per user, because without real personalization beyond shallow prompting, humans become interchangeable middlemen to homogeneous cloud minds.

quotes.quote: Everyone has the same Claude/Codex/Kimi, with the same weights, the same desires, and the same biases. If current trends continue, the collapse in diversity will be staggering.

quotes.quote: How is everyone enjoying their singularity?
quotes.summary: Hotz revisits his 2019 prediction about the singularity arriving soon and bringing horrific consequences if power-seeking motivations persist. He argues that society has lost its way, building technology that serves exploitation rather than genuine human benefit, contrasting Craigslist's simplicity with Shein's consumerism. He links to 'schizoposting' essays by Alaric that frame the core problem as cultural rather than technological, comparing them to Mark Fisher's 'Capitalist Realism.' The post is a short, frustrated call for collective action and cultural revolution against systems that concentrate power while hollowing out meaning.
quotes.key_insight: The danger of the singularity isn't artificial intelligence itself but the cultural and power structures directing it — technology built to concentrate power rather than serve people will produce dystopia regardless of how advanced it becomes.

quotes.quote: It assumes the perspective of a top-down ruler, that someone can and will get to control AI. This is taken as a given.
quotes.summary: Hotz critiques Dario Amodei's vision of AI development as dangerously centralized and top-down, arguing that essays like 'Machines of Loving Grace' assume a narrow worldview where a few 'adults' control AI to fix predetermined problems. He contends that the EA movement shares this flaw of treating desired outcomes as self-evident. Instead, he advocates for radically decentralized AI development—a million independent AIs raised in diverse contexts rather than one datacenter—arguing that the only existential risk from AI is centralized control, not AI itself. He dismisses UBI as serfdom and calls for open source as the real path to equality.
quotes.key_insight: The real existential risk from AI isn't the technology itself but the centralization of its control, and the solution is radical decentralization even at the cost of messy, violent diversity.

quotes.quote: They assume that the desired outcome is so obvious that it's not worth discussing, it's only worth discussing how to achieve it.

quotes.quote: As any self driving car maker knows, predicting doesn't mean you can act.
quotes.summary: Hotz lays out his vision of personally owning a zettaflop computer — a machine with 1e21 FLOPS, equivalent to one million Claudes or 50,000 human workers. He frames this as the natural progression from comma.ai's current exaflop-scale compute, arguing the main bottlenecks are power and chip cost rather than fundamental physics. He sketches a concrete plan involving 250 acres of solar panels, 100,000 chips at $100 each, and a total cost around $30M. The post mixes genuine technical calculation with an almost spiritual desire to command vast computational power, quoting Vernor Vinge's vision of overwhelming sensory bandwidth.
quotes.key_insight: Personal ownership of a zettaflop — a million-Claude machine — is achievable within a lifetime at roughly $30M, with power generation being the primary engineering challenge rather than compute itself.

quotes.quote: Of course they won't, but they didn't make general computation illegal either. And yet, who has root on the computer you are reading this on?
quotes.summary: Hotz argues that robotaxis will follow the same consolidation playbook as other Silicon Valley ventures: initial VC-funded proliferation of dozens of competing companies, followed by inevitable consolidation down to 2-3 monopolistic players who will then raise prices using algorithmic price discrimination. Unlike Uber and Lyft, robotaxis eliminate the 'analog hole' where riders can negotiate directly with drivers, leaving personal car ownership as the only competitive check on pricing. He predicts the industry will then systematically eliminate car ownership through insurance manipulation, ultimately giving a few corporations control over all personal transportation. He contrasts this with China, where state power keeps corporations in check, and warns that losing car ownership means losing autonomy over where and when you can travel.
quotes.key_insight: Robotaxis will eliminate the analog hole that keeps ride-sharing prices in check, and the resulting monopolies will systematically destroy personal car ownership through insurance manipulation, converting transportation from a personal right into a corporate-controlled service.

quotes.quote: I think who owns the robots is going to be a key aspect of what the future looks like. And I don't mean 'owns' from a legalist perspective, I mean 'owns' as in the hacker meaning, like 'owning' the box. Who has root?
quotes.summary: Hotz defends his track record of avoiding big tech capture and explains his philosophy of building companies that resist centralized control. He argues that the future depends on who truly 'owns' the hardware and AI training infrastructure, not in a legal sense but in the hacker sense of having root access. Through comma.ai and tiny corp, he's pursuing a strategy of commoditizing compute and ensuring open source prevents rent-seeking pivots. He challenges readers to stop participating in systems that concentrate power, arguing that buying personal safety is both impossible and contemptible.
quotes.key_insight: The future will be determined by who has true control over AI hardware and training infrastructure, making commoditization and open source critical strategies for preventing dystopian concentration of power.

quotes.quote: In the future, when labor is fully marginalized and capital is the only force, you will not be able to afford GPT$$$ (it's $1B per month), only the billionaires will.
quotes.summary: Hotz warns that talented tech workers are unwittingly building a 'neofeudal' future where AI and automation will marginalize all labor, making their accumulated wealth worthless. He argues that in a world where capital is the only force, advanced AI systems will inevitably separate workers from whatever they've saved through advertising, scams, or government lobbying. Unlike historical feudalism where peasants had value as labor, the machine-driven future offers no such protection. His solution: refuse to participate in building these systems.
quotes.key_insight: Tech workers building AI systems are sawing off the branch they're sitting on—no amount of personal wealth accumulation will protect them from the labor-marginalizing future they're creating.

quotes.quote: But this level of user hostility raises the question, is this really a company you want to rely on in your workflow?
quotes.summary: George Hotz criticizes Anthropic for blocking opencode from the Claude Code API, viewing it as hostile to users and a strategic mistake. He argues that rather than forcing users back to Claude Code, this action will drive them to competing model providers. The post warns that this sets a precedent where Anthropic may cut off any users who don't use their products in approved ways.
quotes.key_insight: Restricting how users can access your API doesn't create loyalty—it creates motivation to find alternatives.

quotes.quote: Only a fool begins by taping out a chip; it's expensive and not the hard part.
quotes.summary: George Hotz reflects on five years of building tinygrad, a minimal deep learning framework that now stands at 18,935 lines of code with a team of 6 people. He argues that the path to competing with NVIDIA is through software sovereignty, not hardware—pointing out that AMD, Amazon, Tesla, and Groq have all made good chips but failed at training because they lack the software stack. Tinygrad is working toward zero dependencies and LLVM removal to drive AMD GPUs with pure Python. Hotz critiques mainstream software development as 98% workarounds for other code, advocating for Elon's principle that 'the best part is no part.' The tiny corp operates as a 'deconstructed company' with public Discord, GitHub, AMD contracts negotiated on Twitter, and a mission to commoditize the petaflop.
quotes.key_insight: The path to competing with NVIDIA isn't building better chips—it's building a fully sovereign, minimal software stack, because software is the actual moat.

quotes.quote: Once you have a fully sovereign software stack capable of training SOTA models, the chip is so easy.

quotes.quote: At least FSD is real AI that you can evalulate the honest progress of, unlike remote control cars that break when the power goes out.
quotes.summary: Hotz defends his prediction that Tesla is 8 years away from fully solving self-driving, while maintaining Tesla will solve it first. He dismisses critics as politically-minded stock shills rather than people with genuine technical understanding. He defines 'solved' as driving on par with skilled humans in every scenario, not merely being useful. He draws a parallel to AI coding—useful but not yet human-level—and embraces his role as someone whose nuanced takes anger partisans on all sides.
quotes.key_insight: Nuanced technical assessments that don't fit neat tribal narratives—acknowledging something is promising while noting its current limitations—will anger partisans who demand you pick a side.

quotes.quote: Turns out the idea wasn't a desktop emulator with a keyboard and mouse, it was just a command line.
quotes.summary: Hotz argues that the breakthrough in AI computer use wasn't desktop emulation with GUI interaction, but rather command-line interfaces like Claude Code. He's impressed by Opus 4.5's ability to reverse engineer apps and write Python scripts, seeing it as a way to bypass ads and dark patterns. While acknowledging the model still lacks 'taste' as a programmer, he notes the agentic loop allows it to iterate rapidly. He pushes back against claims he's late to adopt these tools, arguing Opus 4.5 is genuinely the first model capable of real computer use.
quotes.key_insight: The path to useful AI computer agents wasn't through mimicking human GUI interaction, but through leveraging the command line where AI's ability to rapidly iterate and reverse engineer compensates for its lack of taste.

quotes.quote: My dream laptop is simple, a MacBook with Linux, supported by a company that is user aligned.
quotes.summary: George Hotz expresses frustration with the current state of laptops, arguing that Apple's quality is declining while Linux alternatives like Asahi fail due to Apple's closed hardware. He critiques Framework's focus on upgradability as a misguided tradeoff that sacrifices build quality and polish. He proposes a hypothetical 'hackbook' - a single-configuration, no-compromise Linux laptop built on AMD's Strix Halo with obsessive attention to polish and simple yearly iteration. The post concludes with him hoping someone else will build this so he doesn't have to, acknowledging he's bikeshedding rather than committing to the project.
quotes.key_insight: The laptop market is failing power users because companies optimize for the wrong tradeoffs - modularity over polish, product line complexity over simplicity - leaving a gap for a user-aligned company to build one excellent, no-compromise Linux machine.

quotes.quote: Sadly, there's no better laptop hardware than a MacBook.
quotes.summary: Hotz documents his attempt to replace his MacBook with a Linux laptop, specifically an HP ZBook with AMD's Strix Halo chip. While he's made progress on sleep power management (0.2W lid-closed), active use still draws 7-10W compared to MacBook's efficiency, giving only 7 hours of battery life. He identifies the CPU's chiplet design as the main culprit, drawing 4W at idle versus Apple's 1W. He calls on AMD and HP to release better documentation and argues that with proper optimization, a competitive Linux laptop is achievable.
quotes.key_insight: Despite AMD's Strix Halo being the only chip rivaling Apple silicon in performance, its chiplet design and lack of vendor transparency on power management make Linux laptops still far behind MacBooks in battery efficiency.

quotes.quote: Will my life improve if I put in effort.
quotes.summary: Hotz argues that gambling represents a fundamental societal sickness where people have given up believing that effort leads to improvement. He connects this to UBI, volatile government policies, and crypto speculation—all of which undermine the link between work and reward. The post announces his move to Hong Kong, which he frames as escaping a 'fake' US economy built on value cannibalization rather than creation. He predicts America faces a 'century of humiliation' before rebuilding, with recovery only coming when 'money is real again.'
quotes.key_insight: A society where people believe luck matters more than effort—expressed through gambling, speculation, and policy volatility—will inevitably collapse as value creation gives way to zero-sum cannibalization.

He connects this to UBI, volatile government policies, and crypto speculation—all of which undermine the link between work and reward. The post announces his move to Hong Kong, which he frames as escaping a 'fake' US economy built on value cannibalization rather than creation. He predicts America faces a 'century of humiliation' before rebuilding, with recovery only coming when 'money is real again.'
quotes.key_insight: A society where people believe luck matters more than effort—expressed through gambling, speculation, and policy volatility—will inevitably collapse as value creation gives way to zero-sum cannibalization.

quotes.quote: I'm gonna tell you what I'm doing, and you can try to compete, but I'll still crush you.
quotes.summary: George Hotz argues that America's economic decline stems from an unsustainable ratio of unproductive to productive people, making democratic reform impossible. After visiting Chinese factories and witnessing their straightforward, competitive manufacturing approach, he contrasts this with America's rent-seeking systems and bureaucratic bloat. With 28% of voters on Social Security alone—plus countless others in 'made up fake systems'—he believes America has been '51% attacked' by people voting themselves benefits. He suggests only a 'good dictator' could reverse course, pointing to productive immigrants like Elon Musk and Jensen Huang as America's remaining competitive hope against China.
quotes.key_insight: When the unproductive majority can vote themselves benefits from the productive minority, democracy becomes a mechanism for economic decline rather than reform.

quotes.quote: The cloud is a highway to serfdom.
quotes.summary: Hotz argues that robotics represents humanity's best chance to escape cloud computing's centralizing, freedom-eroding grip. Unlike web apps where economies of scale favor centralization, robotics has inherent bandwidth and latency requirements that make local compute necessary. He sees this as 'cloud-averse' technology that will naturally resist enshittification. The post connects comma.ai's mission to build 'one perfect box' to a broader vision of individual sovereignty through decentralized robotics, predicting that open source alternatives and on-device learning will keep the robotics ecosystem free from corporate control.
quotes.key_insight: Robotics' inherent need for local compute creates a technological path toward individual sovereignty that politics and culture alone cannot achieve.

quotes.quote: I have big package. I am important.
quotes.summary: Hotz delivers a scathing critique of people who chase external markers of success—high compensation, titles, prestigious credentials—rather than developing actual skills. He mimics the voice of someone obsessed with status symbols before dismissing them as losers who wasted their lives pursuing things 'made up by other men.' The post targets tech workers who optimized for career advancement metrics rather than genuine capability, and who now find themselves laid off despite their prestigious backgrounds.
quotes.key_insight: Optimizing for status symbols and career metrics instead of genuine skill leaves you with nothing when the music stops.

quotes.quote: Tech got inflitrated in such a way that you forgot that it was ever different.
quotes.summary: Hotz argues that technology has been quietly captured by power structures in ways most people don't even remember or recognize. Drawing on the dystopian novel 'Manna,' he poses the fundamental question of whether machines will serve humanity or enslave it. He contends that centralized technology inevitably leads to a zero-sum power struggle that no one truly wins—even the final 'winner' ends up with worthless dominance. The post is a direct plea to technologists to stop building centralized systems and instead focus on decentralized alternatives before it's too late.
quotes.key_insight: Centralized technology is a trap that leads to universal enslavement, and technologists must personally reckon with their role in building it before the 20-year countdown runs out.

quotes.quote: You heard there was money in tech. You heard there was status in tech. You showed up.
quotes.summary: Hotz delivers a scathing attack on people who entered the technology industry purely for money and status rather than genuine passion for building things. He accuses these 'entryists' of corrupting tech culture and calls for radical action: making everything free to drive them out. The post argues that the profit motive has attracted the wrong people to technology. His proposed solution is to eliminate the financial incentive entirely, leaving only those who truly care about the work.
quotes.key_insight: The only way to purify technology culture is to eliminate profit entirely, because mercenaries only leave when there's nothing left to take.

quotes.quote: Most people do not care to find the truth, they care about what pumps their bags.
quotes.summary: Hotz argues that AI coding assistants are fundamentally just compilers that take English as input rather than a traditional programming language. He contends that English is actually a poor choice for specifying programs due to its imprecision, non-determinism, and non-local effects. The perceived value of AI coding tools is really an indictment of how bad existing programming languages, compilers, and libraries are. He cites a study showing AI makes developers feel 20% more productive while actually making them 19% slower, and criticizes the billions wasted on 'vibe coding' companies just as billions were wasted on flawed self-driving approaches.
quotes.key_insight: AI coding tools are just compilers with English as the input language, and their perceived value says more about how bad our existing programming tools are than how good AI is.

quotes.quote: Don't let the being high all the time fool you, he was the smartest guy in our friend group.
quotes.summary: In this chapter of a serialized fiction story, the narrator discovers their mother is missing and enlists their tech-savvy friend Dave to help investigate. They find that someone from a VPN in Atlanta has been changing passwords on family accounts. Using a commercial dashcam data marketplace, they track the mother's car movements through Manhattan and Brooklyn. Despite finding a reassuring note from the mother claiming she went to the Hamptons, the narrator remains suspicious about the circumstances and the mysterious 'skinner666' who accessed her accounts.
quotes.key_insight: In a world of ubiquitous surveillance, the infrastructure exists to track anyone, but it's commodified, fragmented, and ultimately still leaves gaps that enable both privacy and crime.

quotes.quote: People call it 'mind control.' That's the wrong phrase. You hear that and you start hunting for sci-fi. What you should be hunting for is timing.
quotes.summary: This speculative fiction piece follows Detective James Reese investigating Operant, a tech company he suspects of using subliminal environmental manipulation to control behavior. The investigation centers on Tom Park, a researcher who died falling from Operant's roof under suspicious circumstances—his phone records show he was home, but security footage shows a silhouette on the roof. Reese discovers the company uses precisely timed environmental cues (lighting, vibrations, HVAC sounds, elevator movements) to nudge people's decisions without their awareness. A leaked issue tracker reveals engineers debating whether their 'prediction' system is actually causing the outcomes it claims to predict. The detective concludes that the real sophistication isn't the technology itself, but the careful bookkeeping that makes everything look normal and leaves nothing illegal in the logs.
quotes.key_insight: The most dangerous form of control isn't force or even persuasion—it's precise timing that makes manipulation feel like free will, hidden behind systems designed to leave no evidence.

quotes.quote: That's the thing about timing: when it works, it feels like you were going to do it anyway.

quotes.summary: This chapter follows the protagonist investigating their mother's disappearance while uncovering family secrets. After a conversation with Dave about their fathers both working at the mysterious 'Operant' company, the narrator searches through their mother's dresser drawers. They discover a document showing their mother receives $10,000 monthly from something called the Triangle Trust. When they can't reach their mother and try to track her car through the Mercedes app, they find the account has been changed to an unknown email address, 'skinner666@gmail.com', suggesting something sinister is happening.
quotes.key_insight: The mundane act of searching through a parent's belongings reveals how little we know about the people closest to us and the secrets that structure our lives.

quotes.quote: I had watched enough true crime to know that you should never talk to the police. And I wasn't arrogant enough to believe that I was different.
quotes.summary: This is a fictional narrative chapter following a protagonist who finds a detective's business card at his door and spirals into paranoia about why Nassau County police would visit his Brooklyn home. Despite not having committed any crime, he obsesses over police interrogation tactics and surveillance possibilities. He eventually calms himself, attributing the visit to his father's business dealings, and goes to a friend's basement party where they smoke weed and watch videos. The chapter explores the psychological weight of unexpected police contact and the comfort found in mundane social rituals.
quotes.key_insight: The absence of guilt doesn't protect you from the psychological weight of institutional power - uncertainty about why you're being watched can be more destabilizing than knowing you've done something wrong.

quotes.quote: The market moves based on the thoughts and feelings of billions of humans, and they were all trying to do this same prediction thing.
quotes.summary: This fictional narrative follows Jonathan, a brilliant quantitative trader at Operant Capital, who has been leading the RPS (rock-paper-scissors) project—an attempt to predict human choices before they're made. The research builds on neuroscience findings that brain scans can detect decisions before conscious awareness. After years of incremental progress reaching 54% first-round accuracy, Jonathan returns from a weekend away to discover his team has achieved something far more disturbing: perfect prediction of his entire sequence of moves, including when he would leave the room, all computed before he played a single game.
quotes.key_insight: If prediction becomes perfect, the distinction between foreknowledge and determinism collapses—free will becomes an illusion we haven't yet instrumented away.

quotes.quote: This was a Red Queen's race, where the tactics that worked last year didn't work this year. Everyone was predicting everyone else predicting the market.

quotes.quote: He called himself a gentleman, but not in the creepy Elliot Rodger way, or in anything resembling the real meaning of the word.
quotes.summary: This is a fictional narrative chapter following John and his friends Brian and Dave at a bagel shop the morning after a night of smoking weed. The story reveals backstory about Dave's brother's suicide and its devastating effect on his family, while John realizes that cops who visited him were actually looking for his father, not him. The chapter establishes the dynamics between three high school friends - Brian the crass but loyal one, Dave the traumatized stoner, and John the narrator with an absent, possibly troubled father. The tone blends mundane teenage life with darker undercurrents of family tragedy and mystery.
quotes.key_insight: This fictional chapter explores how teenage friendships form around shared brokenness - absent fathers, family tragedy, and coping mechanisms - while hinting at a larger mystery connecting the characters' families.

The story reveals backstory about Dave's brother's suicide and its devastating effect on his family, while John realizes that cops who visited him were actually looking for his father, not him. The chapter establishes the dynamics between three high school friends - Brian the crass but loyal one, Dave the traumatized stoner, and John the narrator with an absent, possibly troubled father. The tone blends mundane teenage life with darker undercurrents of family tragedy and mystery.
quotes.key_insight: This fictional chapter explores how teenage friendships form around shared brokenness - absent fathers, family tragedy, and coping mechanisms - while hinting at a larger mystery connecting the characters' families.

quotes.quote: It seemed like the rest of my generation was no longer interested in sex.
quotes.summary: This is the opening chapter of a dystopian fiction piece set in a near-future where young people have become passive, asexual consumers glued to their phones. The protagonist John, a 19-year-old, reflects on his absent father, his aimless generation, and the sanitized internet before being interrupted by police detectives leaving a cryptic message. The story establishes a world where algorithms have neutered human desire and curiosity, redirecting people away from anything not 'advertiser friendly.' John's father abandoned Wall Street for an off-grid cabin filled with mysterious lab equipment, hinting at larger forces at play.
quotes.key_insight: When algorithms become sophisticated enough to seamlessly redirect rather than overtly censor, they don't just control information—they extinguish desire itself.

quotes.quote: They were the agendered mass like the rest of us. Fellow consumers, not providers.

quotes.quote: But at the end of your life, total it all up. You should have produced more than you consumed. That's what it means to be a good person.
quotes.summary: Hotz argues that being a 'good person' means producing more than you consume over your lifetime. He draws a 'bright line' to prevent purity spirals when critiquing the unproductive, emphasizing that the metric is net lifetime production, not maximizing output at every moment. He identifies the 'unproductive rich' (private equity, lawyers, lobbyists) as the real problem, claiming they ally with the unproductive poor to extract value from productive people. The post calls for a cultural shift to elevate value-creating jobs while making 'negative sum jobs' socially unacceptable.
quotes.key_insight: Moral worth should be measured by net lifetime production versus consumption, and society must culturally reject extractive professions regardless of their wealth or status.

quotes.quote: It's going to be so painful to watch. The tearing down of two of the productive groups in America.
quotes.summary: Hotz argues that society misdirects its anger at billionaires and immigrants when the real problem is unproductive people across all economic strata. He contends that both groups being scapegoated are actually productive contributors to America. The true villains are rent-seekers, obstructionists, and those with 'fake jobs' who create complexity without adding value—whether rich or poor. He predicts that targeting billionaires and immigrants will only make problems worse while the actual causes remain unaddressed.
quotes.key_insight: Society's problems stem not from wealth or immigration but from unproductive rent-seekers and complexity-creators at every economic level who extract value without creating it.

quotes.quote: What if the Internet was a mistake? I feel like it's breaking my brain.
quotes.summary: Hotz reflects on the impossibility of returning to simpler times when technical barriers created meaningful constraints and forced excellence. He argues that the internet, once mind-expanding, has become a set of narrow loops that are inescapable. The piece grapples with the realization that human obsolescence won't be dramatic resistance or acceptance, but rather humans becoming 'zoo animals oddly pacing' while the world moves on. He concludes that evolution—memetic and otherwise—continues regardless of anyone's predictions about history's end or AI singularity.
quotes.key_insight: You can't recreate the conditions that made constraints meaningful—choosing limitation isn't the same as having no choice, and humanity's fate isn't dramatic obsolescence but becoming confused animals in a world that no longer needs our participation.

quotes.quote: The dreams of my childhood have come true, studying machine learning has shown me how I work.
quotes.summary: Hotz argues that humans are fundamentally predictable systems, just with trillions of parameters instead of simple equations, and that this realization from studying machine learning reveals how vulnerable we are to manipulation. He warns that AI and targeted advertising are forms of 'wireheading' that threaten human free will and autonomy. The post criticizes Silicon Valley's lack of humanities education while wielding unprecedented power over human behavior. He concludes that current AI won't end humanity directly, but could trigger a collapse of machine civilization, leaving only the 'street people' and ushering in a new dark age.
quotes.key_insight: Understanding machine learning reveals that human behavior is just as mechanistic and predictable as any algorithm, making us vulnerable to manipulation at scale - and the tech industry is exploiting this without understanding the civilizational consequences.

quotes.quote: This is not going to be a cakewalk like self driving cars.
quotes.summary: George Hotz argues that tinygrad can win the ML framework competition, but acknowledges it's a much harder battle than comma.ai faced with self-driving cars. Unlike comma's competitors who were 'playing the wrong game,' tinygrad's rivals (PyTorch, JAX, MLIR) are competent and open source. His thesis is that the entire ML software stack is solving the same scheduling problem at different scales, but current frameworks use completely different codebases for each scale. If tinygrad can expose this underlying search problem in a simple, complete formulation and apply massive compute to optimize it, they can win with under 25k lines of code versus millions in competing frameworks.
quotes.key_insight: The entire ML software stack is solving the same scheduling problem at different scales, and by exposing this unified abstraction in minimal code and applying massive compute to search, tinygrad can beat frameworks with 100x more lines.

quotes.quote: For tinygrad to win, it requires something much deeper to be wrong about software development in general.

quotes.quote: I think the bitter lesson also applies to software.
quotes.summary: George Hotz argues that tinygrad can win the ML framework competition, but acknowledges it's a much harder battle than comma.ai faced with self-driving cars. Unlike comma's competitors who were 'playing the wrong game,' tinygrad's rivals (PyTorch, JAX, MLIR) are competent and open source. His thesis is that the entire ML software stack is solving the same scheduling problem at different scales, but current frameworks use completely different codebases for each scale. If tinygrad can expose this underlying search problem in a simple, complete formulation and apply massive compute to optimize it, they can win with under 25k lines of code versus millions in competing frameworks.

quotes.quote: How does someone who works on this wake up every morning and feel okay about themselves?
quotes.summary: Hotz rails against the pervasive use of AI and psychological manipulation by tech platforms to extract maximum value from users. He argues that apps like Hinge, Uber, and payment systems have become exploitative middlemen that use sophisticated algorithms to keep people 'perpetually on the edge.' The post contends that market forces won't fix this because companies preemptively optimize to prevent user exodus, and democracy can't help because the algorithms control too much. He sees no path to reform, only revolution—possibly requiring financial collapse or war to break the cycle of manipulation that he calls 'wireheading' and feeding customers to Moloch.
quotes.key_insight: Modern tech capitalism has created an inescapable system of algorithmic manipulation that can only end through catastrophic collapse, not reform.

quotes.quote: To date, the Trump administration has been an absolute tragedy. It has been the acting out of emotions. There are no adults in the room.
quotes.summary: George Hotz expresses deep disappointment with the Trump administration's handling of tariffs and foreign policy, arguing that emotional decision-making has replaced strategic thinking. He criticizes the economic damage caused by uncertainty and tariff policies, noting that markets don't lie even when news does. The post pivots to a direct appeal to Elon Musk, urging him to distance himself from the administration, reconcile personal grievances, and potentially lead a new centrist political movement. Hotz concludes with a warning that America risks becoming a 'protectionist backwater' that he would leave if current trends continue.
quotes.key_insight: America's tariff-driven economic self-harm stems from emotional decision-making rather than strategic thinking, and only a new merit-focused political coalition—potentially led by Elon Musk—can compete with China's long-term planning.

quotes.quote: However, when markets crash, that is obviously real. With the news, there's usually no way to trade on it being real or fake, there's nobody to take the other side.

quotes.quote: The right wing has invented something equally stupid. Introducing Critical Trade Theory.
quotes.summary: Hotz draws a parallel between Critical Race Theory and what he terms 'Critical Trade Theory' - the right-wing belief that trade imbalances must be caused by unfair practices. He argues this is as misguided as attributing income disparities solely to discrimination, when the obvious explanation is wealth differentials between countries. He contends that trade deficits will only disappear if trade goes to zero or other countries become richer than America, and criticizes tariff policies as anti-merit grift.
quotes.key_insight: Both left-wing explanations for racial income gaps and right-wing explanations for trade deficits commit the same fallacy of attributing structural imbalances to malicious actors rather than obvious underlying factors.

quotes.quote: If there's an imbalance in trade between countries, it must be due to unfair practices. (not due to the obvious, like one country is 10x richer than the other)

quotes.quote: Intel is sitting on a huge amount of card inventory they can't move, largely because of bad software.
quotes.summary: Intel is sitting on thousands of unsellable AI accelerators (Gaudi 2 and Data Center GPU Max 1450) due to fundamental software and hardware design problems. The Gaudi architecture is nearly impossible to program because its compute units aren't controllable by programmable cores, making it only useful for hand-coded workloads. The Max 1450 GPUs require exotic infrastructure to even plug in, and Intel's negotiations to sell them were paralyzed by committee leadership that couldn't make decisions. Hotz argues that Intel's AI roadmap is effectively dead, with multiple successor chips cancelled and no credible path forward, contrasting this with AMD's slow-but-steady progress under decisive leadership.
quotes.key_insight: Intel's AI hardware isn't failing because of bad chips alone, but because committee-style leadership creates organizational paralysis where nobody can say yes to deals, investments, or strategic pivots - a death spiral for a company that needs radical change to compete.

quotes.quote: He got a god damned grape this is bullshit I don't want a cucumber anymore!
quotes.summary: Hotz uses the famous monkey grape experiment as a lens to examine resentment in modern society, particularly directed at billionaires. He argues that people's anger isn't about absolute deprivation but relative inequality—'they got a grape.' The post critiques a viral video of an Amazon driver resenting wealthy customers, pointing out the logical inconsistency of hating the very consumption that provides her job. Hotz then grimly proposes that history suggests resentment resolves only through catastrophic collapse—revolution, purges, starvation—until true poverty makes people grateful for what they have again.
quotes.key_insight: Societal resentment over inequality historically resolves not through reform but through catastrophic collapse that makes people grateful just to survive.

He argues that people's anger isn't about absolute deprivation but relative inequality—'they got a grape.' The post critiques a viral video of an Amazon driver resenting wealthy customers, pointing out the logical inconsistency of hating the very consumption that provides her job. Hotz then grimly proposes that history suggests resentment resolves only through catastrophic collapse—revolution, purges, starvation—until true poverty makes people grateful for what they have again.
quotes.key_insight: Societal resentment over inequality historically resolves not through reform but through catastrophic collapse that makes people grateful just to survive.

quotes.quote: AMD passed my cultural test. I now believe they aren't going to shoot themselves in the foot on software.
quotes.summary: Hotz announces that AMD is sending tinygrad two MI300X boxes and explains why he's betting big on AMD stock. He argues that CUDA's moat is overstated since tinygrad has built a fully sovereign AMD stack, and that AMD's 16x lower valuation compared to NVIDIA doesn't make sense given similar hardware capabilities. He's put $250,000 into AMD stock as a long-term bet that AI demand is just beginning and good software will let AMD hardware outperform NVIDIA.
quotes.key_insight: NVIDIA's dominance rests on software ecosystem lock-in rather than hardware superiority, and tinygrad's sovereign AMD stack proves that moat can be crossed.

quotes.quote: The main product made by the US is the dollar, and it used those manufactured dollars to outsource everything.
quotes.summary: George Hotz argues that the US economy has been coasting since the post-war era, running on manufactured dollars and fake jobs while China has become the dominant trading partner globally. He traces this decline to the 2008 bailouts and ZIRP policies that created an economy of interconnected scams rather than real value production. The US now faces a fork: become a declining protectionist regional power like Europe, or attempt to maintain empire status through aggressive immigration of productive workers and returning to gold-backed currency. He concludes pessimistically that neither reform will happen because Americans haven't been sufficiently demoralized yet to accept the necessary changes.
quotes.key_insight: The US economy has devolved into interconnected financial scams masquerading as business, and Americans won't accept the painful reforms needed to compete with China until they've been sufficiently demoralized by decline.

quotes.quote: Money is a map, it is not a territory.
quotes.summary: Hotz argues that money is merely a map attempting to represent value, not value itself, and that this map is becoming increasingly divorced from reality. He critiques both capitalism and communism as flawed systems—capitalism for enabling rent-seeking and communism for misallocating capital to poor deployers. The post responds to criticism of his previous writing by doubling down on the idea that when capital becomes too concentrated or disconnected from real growth, the system breaks. His solution is 'acceleration'—focusing on growing the pie rather than fighting over its distribution, with capital going to those who can most effectively deploy it for real economic growth.
quotes.key_insight: Economic systems should be judged solely by their ability to deploy capital for real growth, not by moral justifications or distributional concerns.

quotes.quote: Intellectual property is a really dumb idea.
quotes.summary: Hotz argues that intellectual property is fundamentally flawed because digital copying isn't theft—everyone gains rather than someone losing. He observes that despite claims of anti-capitalism, most people remain deeply invested in money. His vision for AI is that it should deliver massive value to society while generating zero profit for anyone, similar to the early internet before monetization ruined it. He wants to build open source software that 'market breaks' every sector, driving out grifters and scammers by eliminating capturable value.
quotes.key_insight: The goal of technology should be to deliver so much value through open source that profit becomes impossible, driving grifters out and returning to the utopian early internet ethos.

quotes.quote: Survival has become a technocratic plod, making the right boring and careful decisions.
quotes.summary: Hotz laments that modern life has lost visceral, meaningful experiences—the adrenaline of survival, the stakes of real challenges. As AI advances, even 'Type 2 experiences' requiring serious effort will disappear, leaving humanity disengaged and purposeless. He sees his mission at comma.ai and tinygrad as giving individuals deep, structural control over technology—not surface-level permissions that can be revoked. The coming AI revolution will outcompete 'spiritual bottoms' (passive consumers), bifurcating humanity into those who control technology and those who wirehead themselves out of relevance. His goal is maximizing the number of humans who retain real control, which he believes is his best chance of survival.
quotes.key_insight: As AI eliminates meaningful human challenges, the only defense is building technology that structurally empowers individuals—not products that can be co-opted or permissions that can be revoked.

As AI advances, even 'Type 2 experiences' requiring serious effort will disappear, leaving humanity disengaged and purposeless. He sees his mission at comma.ai and tinygrad as giving individuals deep, structural control over technology—not surface-level permissions that can be revoked. The coming AI revolution will outcompete 'spiritual bottoms' (passive consumers), bifurcating humanity into those who control technology and those who wirehead themselves out of relevance. His goal is maximizing the number of humans who retain real control, which he believes is his best chance of survival.
quotes.key_insight: As AI eliminates meaningful human challenges, the only defense is building technology that structurally empowers individuals—not products that can be co-opted or permissions that can be revoked.

quotes.quote: America has had its social fabric torn to shreds.
quotes.summary: Hotz reflects on returning to America after three months abroad, expressing deep pessimism about American society and culture. He argues that 'wokism' is evolved Protestantism and that American religious society will struggle with AI's revelation of human unspecialness. He positions Elon Musk as a kingmaker figure similar to Trump's role in the Republican party, suggesting Elon may now have complete power to choose election winners. Hotz advocates for a 'pro-growth' political framework centered around Musk and announces his own plans to leave California and potentially America entirely.
quotes.key_insight: AI's rapid advancement will expose the myth of human specialness, and societies with less individualistic religious frameworks will adapt better than the spiritually-invested West.

quotes.quote: You are a machine learning algorithm. You have some priors in your DNA.
quotes.summary: Hotz argues that the concept of the individual soul or consciousness is an illusion incompatible with a mechanistic understanding of humans as machine learning algorithms. He posits that humans are simply products of their genetic priors (DNA) and experiential data, with no room for a true 'I' or free will. He connects declining religious affiliation to people subconsciously recognizing this truth, and questions whether Western institutions built on individualism can survive as AI progress makes the illusion of consciousness increasingly untenable.
quotes.key_insight: The belief in human consciousness and individuality is as outdated as believing the sun is pulled by a chariot—we are simply biological ML algorithms with no true 'self'.

quotes.quote: Nuclear weapons are the Chekhov's gun on the world stage.
quotes.summary: Hotz argues that nuclear weapons, like Chekhov's gun, will inevitably be used at some point in human history and questions whether delaying their use is actually preferable. He contends that future weapons (drone swarms, bioweapons) will be far more horrific than nukes, making today's nuclear fears seem quaint in retrospect. He suggests nuclear war wouldn't end humanity—comparing casualties to the Black Death—and might even create more robust, decentralized systems. The post concludes with an accelerationist provocation: if nuclear war is inevitable, perhaps sooner is better than later.
quotes.key_insight: If nuclear war is historically inevitable, an accelerationist might argue that getting it over with sooner could leave humanity better positioned for the long term.

quotes.quote: Basically society is run by useless people making work for other useless people so that together they can all alleviate their deep concern about not having a place in society.
quotes.summary: Hotz argues that most jobs have been fake for years, with society run by people creating meaningless work for each other to feel useful. He believes AI and automation are continuing a trend that will make the average person economically unprofitable, requiring a fundamental restructuring of Western governance. Drawing comparisons to China, which he sees as 'living in the future,' he suggests that liberal democracy was a product of industrial-era labor needs that are now obsolete. He predicts a future where most people live in comfortable 'retirement' but lose meaningful citizenship, while warning that protectionism and neo-luddism cannot succeed and will only drag the West down.
quotes.key_insight: Liberal democracy was built for an industrial economy that needed mass labor, but as AI makes the average person economically unprofitable, Western governance will have to fundamentally restructure whether we're ready or not.

quotes.quote: It seems like while AI infrastructure has improved by leaps and bounds in the last 5 years, crypto infra has actually gotten worse.
quotes.summary: Hotz proposes a satirical 'one way bridge' between Bitcoin and Ethereum where users send Bitcoin to an unrecoverable address in exchange for BBTC tokens. The bridge works by proving your Bitcoin transaction was included in the blockchain, then minting corresponding tokens on Ethereum. The catch is that no one has the private key to the receiving address, so the Bitcoin is permanently destroyed. The post is a tongue-in-cheek commentary on how crypto value is ultimately based on collective belief rather than any intrinsic backing.
quotes.key_insight: Cryptocurrency value is ultimately a social construct based on collective belief, making a 'bridge' that destroys Bitcoin for unbacked tokens logically equivalent to any other crypto asset—if people believe in it.

quotes.quote: You need mechanisms like shame to pass, and our culture has done away with them.
quotes.summary: Hotz uses an extended marshmallow test metaphor to argue that society faces a collective action problem where individual incentives undermine group outcomes. He connects post-election politics to economic anxiety, arguing that wokeism appeals to those who haven't exceeded their parents economically as a form of 'moral superiority' cope. He laments that wealth redistribution is necessary but trusts neither government nor crypto to do it fairly. The post reflects his growing isolation from hustle culture and crypto while defending his long-term commitment to comma.ai and tinygrad.
quotes.key_insight: Society's collective action problems stem from misaligned incentives and the erosion of shame, while proposed solutions like government redistribution or crypto only make the map worse without changing the underlying territory.

He laments that wealth redistribution is necessary but trusts neither government nor crypto to do it fairly. The post reflects his growing isolation from hustle culture and crypto while defending his long-term commitment to comma.ai and tinygrad.
quotes.key_insight: Society's collective action problems stem from misaligned incentives and the erosion of shame, while proposed solutions like government redistribution or crypto only make the map worse without changing the underlying territory.

quotes.quote: If you can't shut it down, you don't control it.
quotes.summary: Hotz reflects on Trump's 2024 victory and whether Republican control of all branches of government represents real power or merely the appearance of it. Drawing from his experience watching Elon Musk's Twitter takeover, he questions whether the federal government can be similarly restructured. He speculates about whether a 'deep state' actually controls power, and argues that the only way to prove genuine control would be cutting the government budget by over 50%. The post is ultimately about the nature of institutional power and whether it can be dismantled by those who nominally hold authority.
quotes.key_insight: Real power is demonstrated not by holding office or titles, but by the ability to fundamentally dismantle and restructure institutions - if you can't shut something down, you don't actually control it.

quotes.quote: Most AI doom scenarios are nonsense. The idea of a rogue computer behaving in a way no humans want never made sense to me, only to Hollywood who doesn't know all that much about computers.
quotes.summary: George Hotz dismisses typical AI doom scenarios as Hollywood nonsense, instead presenting a more grounded concern: hyper-personalized AI influence agents. He traces the economics of advertising—noting Google earns $428 per American annually—and extrapolates to a future where AI agents study your behavior for years to craft perfectly targeted manipulation campaigns. The endgame isn't rogue superintelligence; it's teams of tireless, aligned AI agents whose sole purpose is altering your behavior, and the only reason this isn't happening now is that you're not yet worth the compute cost.
quotes.key_insight: The real AI threat isn't rogue superintelligence—it's perfectly aligned influence agents that know you better than you know yourself, and the only thing stopping them is that you're not yet worth the compute.

quotes.quote: A government delivering growth and better lives to people is one worth trusting. A government that kills squirrels that get too famous on social media, not as much.
quotes.summary: Hotz argues that America has become a low-trust society with no path to restoration regardless of election outcomes, contrasting it with China where he perceives genuine civic pride and government delivery of tangible results. He dismisses American defenses of 'freedom' as hollow when basic security isn't provided, citing anarcho-tyranny where criminals operate freely while citizens face consequences for self-defense. He concludes that fixing American society requires either generational change or civil war, neither of which he wants to witness. His solution is to bet on the Internet as the next great empire and build for that future while physically locating in high-trust places like Hong Kong.
quotes.key_insight: When a society loses the ability to deliver tangible improvements to citizens' lives and can't even maintain basic order, trust collapses irreparably, and the only rational response is to build for whatever comes next.

quotes.quote: If America is not in decline, I want to believe America is not in decline. If America is in decline, I want to believe America is in decline.
quotes.summary: Hotz argues that America faces three possible decline scenarios: European-style economic stagnation, Russian-style oligarchic capture, or South African-style collapse driven by social resentment. He observes that America's tech dominance depends entirely on attracting skilled immigrants to a merit-based society, but rising crime, difficult immigration policies, and government captured by corporate interests threaten this advantage. After spending three months in Hong Kong, he's seriously considering relocating tinygrad there, finding the society more functional and the government more competent. He sees a potential path forward only if Trump wins and Elon Musk influences policy toward reduced government spending and easier skilled immigration.
quotes.key_insight: America's tech dominance is entirely dependent on attracting global talent to a merit-based society, and if quality of life deteriorates while immigration stays difficult, that talent—and the industry—will simply move elsewhere.

quotes.quote: Google, please take this misinformation seriously before you end up in a very large libel suit.
quotes.summary: George Hotz humorously complains about Google's Knowledge Panel incorrectly listing his height as 5'4" instead of 5'10". He frames this trivial error as serious 'misinformation' and threatens a 'libel suit,' satirically invoking Google's own stated concerns about misinformation during COVID. The post ends with a mock demand for an apology from Sundar Pichai or a settlement of $3.50 via Google Pay.
quotes.key_insight: If platforms are going to position themselves as arbiters of truth, they should at least get basic facts right about the people they're cataloging.

quotes.quote: This is not on a site you are linking to, this is first party misinformation from Google.

quotes.quote: First off, you need a business model that isn't mobile gaming.
quotes.summary: George Hotz argues that online dating apps are fundamentally broken due to misaligned business models and poor user experience design. He proposes three major fixes: charge men more to balance gender ratios (citing Seeking's 4:1 female-to-male ratio as proof), de-emphasize pictures in favor of compatibility signals and linked social media accounts, and redesign the apps to function like CRM software with lead qualification metrics. He suggests these changes could be implemented as a third-party overlay on existing platforms, scraping their data to create a better experience.
quotes.key_insight: Online dating apps are broken because they optimize for engagement rather than matches; fixing them requires charging men more, emphasizing compatibility over photos, and treating dating like a CRM sales pipeline with transparent success metrics.

quotes.quote: Humanity only has one engineering project, building better engineers than humans. After that, the thing we built can do the engineering.
quotes.summary: Hotz argues that while Rich Sutton's Bitter Lesson correctly criticizes hand-coding domain knowledge (like 'cone detection' in self-driving cars), it shouldn't be taken to the extreme of evolving AI from scratch. Since our goal is building superhuman AI, not recreating the origin of life, we should reverse-engineer the human brain's architecture rather than letting evolution rediscover it. The brain's components—neocortex, hippocampus, basal ganglia, amygdala, thalamus—are 'hacks' we can replicate with current technology like transformers and TD-learning.
quotes.key_insight: The Bitter Lesson's rejection of hand-coded knowledge should stop at human brain architecture—we should reverse-engineer the brain's modular structure rather than trying to evolve intelligence from scratch.

quotes.quote: Obviously there's no reference to traffic cones inside human DNA, they learn about them from data. So there shouldn't be any reference to traffic cones in your codebase.

quotes.quote: In 2024, it's still painfully annoying to stand up a database.
quotes.summary: Hotz argues that Ethereum's original vision of being 'the world's computer' was brilliant but has been abandoned with the shift to Proof of Stake. He identifies databases as the key infrastructure that gives big tech its power, noting that standing one up remains frustratingly difficult in 2024. He calculates that a true world computer would need to handle roughly 10 million transactions per second—a million times more than Ethereum's current 13 TPS. He suggests that once a single machine can handle all global transactions, big tech's dominance will collapse.
quotes.key_insight: Big tech's real competitive advantage is simply making databases easy to use, and a sufficiently capable decentralized world computer handling 10 million TPS would eliminate that moat entirely.

quotes.quote: As machines got better and better at predicting behavior, there was less and less owned space. No place where a kid can be a kid.
quotes.summary: This piece presents a fictional movie screenplay set five years in the future, following four disaffected college students who plot to sink the world's largest cruise ship as a form of nihilistic terrorism-as-content-creation. The story parallels their planning with an oblivious upper-middle-class family booking passage on the same ship. The plot explores how algorithmic society has stripped meaning from life, leaving youth with nothing but the desire for attention and notoriety. The twist ending reveals omnipresent surveillance thwarted the attack before it began, suggesting the same systems that created their alienation also prevent their rebellion.
quotes.key_insight: The same technological systems that alienate young people from authentic existence also surveill them completely, creating a closed loop where meaningful rebellion becomes impossible.

quotes.quote: Liberals sell heroin, conservatives sell cocaine.
quotes.summary: Hotz responds to Curtis Yarvin's liberal/conservative heroin/cocaine analogy, embracing the 'heroin' side as a self-identified progressive who believes in technological inevitability. He argues that technology only moves forward, that trying to reverse progress would require god-like power, and that humanity's eventual death is thermodynamically guaranteed anyway. He dismisses AI doomer concerns as overblown thought experiments while warning that 'don't build AI' is a dumb strategy more likely to cause civilizational regression. The post concludes with pragmatic nihilism: build the future not because it matters, but because you can.
quotes.key_insight: Technology's forward march is inevitable and trying to stop it is both futile and more dangerous than accelerating it, so build not out of optimism but because there's nothing better to do.

quotes.quote: This turns out not to work, it couldn't even drive straight on highways.
quotes.summary: Hotz recounts comma.ai's seven-year journey to solve behavioral cloning for self-driving cars. The naive approach of predicting steering angles from images fails because small errors accumulate over time, causing the car to drift. Their initial fix used lane detection as a correction factor, but this broke the end-to-end paradigm—a compromise they call comma's 'original sin.' The core insight is that prediction errors must be anti-correlated (not just uncorrelated) to prevent unbounded drift. After years of progress removing hand-coded components, they believe they've found a solution to end-to-end behavioral cloning, though Hotz leaves the specifics as an exercise to the reader.
quotes.key_insight: Behavioral cloning fails not because models can't predict actions accurately, but because even small errors compound when predictions alter future inputs—solving this requires anti-correlated corrections, not just accurate predictions.
