quotes.quote: This model is easy to train, and can achieve very low losses on a holdout set. However, it won't drive a car.
quotes.summary: Hotz recounts comma.ai's seven-year journey to solve behavioral cloning for self-driving cars. The naive approach of predicting steering angles from images fails because small errors accumulate over time, causing the car to drift. Their initial fix used lane detection as a correction factor, but this broke the end-to-end paradigm—a compromise they call comma's 'original sin.' The core insight is that prediction errors must be anti-correlated (not just uncorrelated) to prevent unbounded drift. After years of progress removing hand-coded components, they believe they've found a solution to end-to-end behavioral cloning, though Hotz leaves the specifics as an exercise to the reader.
quotes.key_insight: Behavioral cloning fails not because models can't predict actions accurately, but because even small errors compound when predictions alter future inputs—solving this requires anti-correlated corrections, not just accurate predictions.

quotes.quote: You disrupt the government by providing 10x better versions of all of the services that it provides.
quotes.summary: The post argues that the most effective way to reduce government power is not through armed revolution but by building decentralized alternatives that outcompete government services. Hotz contends that the US government has been failing since around 1971, pointing to stagnant energy use, ballooning healthcare costs, and ineffective education spending as evidence. He proposes specific disruption strategies: crypto-based payment systems to replace credit cards, private solar grids, AI-powered security systems, and outcome-focused alternatives to education and healthcare. The ultimate goal is creating a society with minimal government reliance, such that any eventual dictator would be weak and ineffectual.
quotes.key_insight: The path to reducing government power isn't revolution but building superior decentralized alternatives that make government services obsolete through competition.

quotes.quote: A free society requires personal responsiblity. People here want to be managed. They want a ruling class.
quotes.summary: Hotz argues that modern democracies are unsustainable because many people prefer being managed over accepting the responsibility that comes with freedom. He proposes a three-class society: Citizens who participate economically and have full political rights, Subjects who receive government support but forfeit voting rights and must live separately, and the Incarcerated. This system would allow people to opt into dependency while preventing them from voting themselves benefits that would bankrupt the state. He believes this is the only way to prevent democratic collapse into dictatorship.
quotes.key_insight: Democracy can only survive if those who choose government dependency voluntarily surrender their political voice, preventing the inevitable vote-yourself-rich spiral that destroys free societies.

quotes.quote: If you are writing 0 bugs, you are coding too slowly, and in a competitive enviroment you will lose.
quotes.summary: Hotz argues against Eliezer Yudkowsky's AI doom predictions, contending that superintelligent AIs will remain inscrutable weight matrices rather than formal systems capable of 'solving' game theory problems like the prisoner's dilemma. He believes the rise of AI will be slow and predictable, similar to the industrial revolution's muscle machines. The brain operates near physical efficiency limits, and current silicon still has significant ground to cover before matching human computational capacity. Rather than fearing rapid takeoff, Hotz predicts a manageable economic acceleration—doubling every 2 years instead of daily—which he views as exciting rather than catastrophic.
quotes.key_insight: Superintelligent AI will be bounded by the same hard computational limits that constrain all systems, making its rise gradual and manageable rather than sudden and catastrophic.

quotes.quote: AI looked scary. It looked like it was one FOOM away from self playing and becoming superhuman at the universe.
quotes.summary: George Hotz argues against the AI 'hard takeoff' or FOOM scenario by distinguishing between closed games like Go and the open complexity of the real universe. While AI achieved superhuman performance in games through self-play, the universe contains computers and other players that must be modeled, making dominance impossible without majority compute. He uses the hedge fund analogy to show that predicting markets requires modeling all other market participants, including their computers. Hotz concludes that compute will naturally distribute in a power law, and the real risk comes from artificial restrictions that could create a 'powder keg' where one actor breaks the rules.
quotes.key_insight: AI can't 'take off' and dominate the universe because unlike closed games, the real world contains other computers and players that must be modeled, ensuring compute advantages remain distributed rather than concentrated.

quotes.quote: If a person has 20 PFLOPS of compute, GPT-4 used 47.5 person-years to train. Very human scale.
quotes.summary: Hotz calculates what it would take to build a computer capable of training GPT-4 in a single day instead of 90 days. Starting from the observation that GPT-4's training used roughly 47.5 person-years of compute, he works through the economics of building such a machine using wafer-scale compute and solar power rather than traditional GPUs and grid electricity. He arrives at a surprisingly feasible $400M budget and frames this as tinygrad's future fundraising goal. The post concludes with a call to find the ideal location for this 'new Los Alamos' of AI compute.
quotes.key_insight: By vertically integrating wafer-scale compute and solar power, you can build a GPT-4-in-a-day training cluster for under $400M instead of billions - making frontier AI training accessible outside hyperscalers.

quotes.quote: In about 15 years, a desktop computer will be able to do everything a human can. In 25, a mobile phone.
quotes.summary: Hotz predicts desktop computers will match human capabilities in 15 years, mobile phones in 25, and argues this should inspire optimism rather than fear. He dismisses concerns about AI existential risk as misguided collectivism, comparing centralized 'singularity' narratives to communism. The Earth is small and limiting, but space and the metaverse offer infinite expansion. Humanity's future will be radically diverse—people can choose struggle, games, or wireheading—and AI is simply another tool for self-actualization.
quotes.key_insight: The real existential risk isn't superintelligent AI but cultural homogenization—humanity's salvation lies in radical diversity of choice across an expanding universe.

quotes.quote: The point of communication is not to convey truth, it's to acheive desired outcomes.
quotes.summary: Hotz reflects on starting tinygrad and his refusal to play the typical startup game of hype over substance. He uses Cruise Automation as a cautionary example of a company that survives by selling perception rather than delivering products, burning $5M daily while promising revenue is 'right around the corner.' He outlines the disgusting but effective playbook: hire aggressively, market before you have a product, sell vaporware, raise more money based on the appearance of success. Despite knowing this playbook works, he refuses to follow it, preferring truth over success.
quotes.key_insight: The startup world rewards perception over reality, and while Hotz understands this playbook perfectly, he considers following it a form of self-destruction worse than failure.

quotes.quote: I know what it takes to succeed, but I'm not willing to do it.

quotes.quote: We are hoping that this will improve your perception of AMD products and this will be reflected in your public messaging.
quotes.summary: Hotz documents his experience getting AMD to acknowledge and fix critical driver issues affecting the 7900XTX GPU. After public complaints, AMD sent him a private ROCm 5.6 driver tarball that resolved the kernel panics and GPU crashes, but asked him not to distribute it. He dives into AMD's confusing dual-driver architecture (amdgpu vs AMDGPU-Pro) and criticizes their closed development workflow. His core argument is that AMD's cathedral-style closed development undermines their potential competitive advantage against NVIDIA.
quotes.key_insight: AMD's nominally open-source drivers developed behind closed doors offer no real advantage over NVIDIA's proprietary approach—true openness requires public development workflows and hardware documentation.

quotes.quote: We sell computers for more than they cost to make; I've been thinking about this one for a while.
quotes.summary: George Hotz announces that his new venture, the tiny corp, has raised $5.1M to commoditize the petaflop and democratize AI compute. The company's strategy is to build superior software (tinygrad) for existing AMD hardware rather than tape out new chips, arguing that previous AI chip startups failed because they neglected software despite having working silicon. Hotz contends that AMD's RDNA3 hardware is excellent but unusable due to terrible software, and the tiny corp will write a complete framework, runtime, and driver from scratch. The immediate goal is to get AMD on MLPerf, with the long-term vision of breaking NVIDIA's monopoly and ensuring AI compute is commoditized across 50 competing companies.
quotes.key_insight: The path to breaking NVIDIA's AI compute monopoly runs through superior open-source software for existing commodity hardware, not through taping out new chips.

quotes.quote: We will define one person of compute as 20 PFLOPS (64 A100s, or a single dense 42U A100 rack).
quotes.summary: Hotz introduces 'person of compute' as a unit of measurement (20 PFLOPS) to contextualize AI computing power in human-scale terms. He calculates that LLaMA took about 2 person-years to train while GPT-4 required roughly 100 person-years. Using Moore's Law projections, he estimates when various scales of compute will become accessible—from Google's current 450-person computer to a hypothetical 'Humanity' of compute (8 billion person-equivalents). He concludes with a timeline suggesting desktop-scale 'Humanity' compute by 2089, expressing impatience with the pace.
quotes.key_insight: Framing compute in human-equivalent units reveals that today's largest AI models are still at comprehensible human scales, but Moore's Law will deliver desktop-level 'Humanity' compute within a lifetime.

Using Moore's Law projections, he estimates when various scales of compute will become accessible—from Google's current 450-person computer to a hypothetical 'Humanity' of compute (8 billion person-equivalents). He concludes with a timeline suggesting desktop-scale 'Humanity' compute by 2089, expressing impatience with the pace.
quotes.key_insight: Framing compute in human-equivalent units reveals that today's largest AI models are still at comprehensible human scales, but Moore's Law will deliver desktop-level 'Humanity' compute within a lifetime.

quotes.quote: There's a fine line that defines civilization.
quotes.summary: Hotz proposes 'Wireheading City,' a radical solution to homelessness: a 5-mile diameter city in Nevada where people can opt out of society entirely. Residents receive free food, shelter, entertainment, and unlimited access to drugs including alcohol, weed, meth, and opioids. The city functions as a voluntary alternative to both homelessness and prison, with strict border controls to prevent drug export. Hotz argues this would reduce prison populations, save money, and accommodate those who don't want to participate in the social contract they never signed.
quotes.key_insight: Rather than forcing everyone into the same social contract, society should create opt-out zones where people can live with basic needs met while pursuing whatever self-destructive behaviors they choose.

quotes.quote: With true equality of opportunity, we will see equality of outcome. If we don't see equality of outcome, it is because we don't have equality of opportunity.
quotes.summary: Hotz attempts to define 'wokeism' by tracing its core belief: that true equality of opportunity would produce equality of outcome, and unequal outcomes prove unequal opportunity. He argues this premise is factually false, not merely a difference of values. Using diversity in tech statistics as his example, he shows how advocates complain about unequal outcomes while claiming to support equality of opportunity. He concludes that this factually false belief functions like a cult doctrine, binding adherents together precisely because it requires faith over evidence.
quotes.key_insight: Wokeism's hidden axiom—that equal opportunity produces equal outcomes—is empirically false, which explains both its predictive failures and its reliance on social enforcement rather than evidence.

quotes.quote: Nobody is blocking highways for accelerationism. But perhaps they should be, acceleration is the greatest form of charity.
quotes.summary: Hotz examines effective accelerationism (e/acc) as a counterpoint to Effective Altruism, arguing that acceleration itself is the greatest form of charity. He critiques process-oriented organizations and the professional managerial class, proposing that Elon Musk's management success comes from creating chaos that forces goal-orientation over process-following. The post culminates in a vision for AI-driven organizations where an artificial intelligence replaces the dictator role, using evaluation functions too complex for humans to game. He frames this as a solution to Goodhart's law and organizational misalignment.
quotes.key_insight: The solution to organizational dysfunction may be replacing human management with AI evaluation functions complex enough to resist Goodhart's law gaming, forcing alignment toward actual goals rather than process compliance.

quotes.quote: Feels like ChatGPT wrote it.
quotes.summary: George Hotz argues that Avatar 2 wasted its massive budget on a beautiful but hollow story, comparing its plot quality to ChatGPT output. He proposes an alternative storyline centered on two brothers with opposing philosophies about violence and pacifism, rather than the original's recycled narrative. His rewrite kills Jake Sully in the first act and explores themes of moral nuance through a conflict between a revenge-seeking warrior brother and a pacifist younger brother who joins the water tribe. The proposed ending features a political council where humans and Na'vi sit together, explicitly rejecting black-and-white thinking. Hotz frames this as 'a needed story for our time' about finding common ground despite conflicting desires.
quotes.key_insight: Great spectacle without meaningful story is a waste, and modern narratives need to move beyond simplistic good-versus-evil framing toward acknowledging moral complexity and the possibility of reconciliation.

quotes.quote: It's no longer a race car, it's a boat. And steering a boat requires too much damn planning and patience.
quotes.summary: George Hotz announces he's stepping back from comma.ai, recognizing that the company has evolved beyond what he's suited to lead. He reflects on the difference between solving problems and running sustainable businesses, noting that comma could become a 100M+ revenue company but requires 'boat steering' rather than his 'race car' style. He introduces tinygrad/Tiny Corporation as a hobby project but dismisses it as historically insignificant. The post takes a philosophical turn as he contemplates embarking on a literal Hero's Journey to find the Garden of Eden, seeking to understand human consciousness and our relationship with machines.
quotes.key_insight: The most important battles aren't technological but existential—understanding what makes humans distinct from machines matters more than building better AI.

quotes.quote: I will die before I give up my hope for technology because that's the only thing I am living for.
quotes.summary: Hotz reflects on societal dysfunction, from patent trolls to the culture war, while grappling with the Unabomber's anti-technology thesis. He rejects Kaczynski's conclusion that technology must be destroyed, clinging instead to hope that technology can be democratized. His central thesis is that AI's future hinges on whether it's compute-limited (favoring centralized powers) or data-limited (enabling individual ownership). If training the best models becomes accessible to individuals using publicly available data, we might achieve a decentralized AI future reminiscent of personal computers in the 1980s.
quotes.key_insight: The fate of human autonomy in the AI age depends on whether intelligence scales with compute (centralization wins) or data (individuals can compete).

quotes.quote: To answer the questions about real AI safety would involve addressing the unabomber manifesto, and I suspect even if you had an answer, in the current world that would speak way too much truth to power to happen.
quotes.summary: Hotz reflects on societal dysfunction, from patent trolls to the culture war, while grappling with the Unabomber's anti-technology thesis. He rejects Kaczynski's conclusion that technology must be destroyed, clinging instead to hope that technology can be democratized. His central thesis is that AI's future hinges on whether it's compute-limited (favoring centralized powers) or data-limited (enabling individual ownership). If training the best models becomes accessible to individuals using publicly available data, we might achieve a decentralized AI future reminiscent of personal computers in the 1980s.

quotes.quote: You are a bunch of randomly initialized dot products with some quasiconvex optimizer that's tricked you into believing you are you.
quotes.summary: On his 33rd birthday, Hotz reflects on consciousness, technological progress, and the inevitability of superhuman AI. He argues that consciousness is merely an illusion created by neural networks, and that technology inexorably moves challenges from difficult to trivial. Drawing on the Unabomber manifesto, he categorizes human drives by difficulty level and predicts AI will eventually relearn and surpass all of human culture, just as AlphaZero did for chess. The post ends as a recruitment pitch for comma.ai, positioning it among the major AI labs pursuing different paths to artificial general intelligence.
quotes.key_insight: Superhuman AI is inevitable and will not merely match human capabilities but completely relearn and transcend human culture itself, rendering 'human level' a meaningless waypoint.

quotes.quote: Before asking the question, how do I build AGI, you first must ask the question, what would I recognize as AGI?
quotes.summary: Hotz argues that AGI should be defined by its ability to take almost any human job, which requires human-like embodiment and the crucial ability to learn on-device in real-world conditions. He dismisses simulation-based approaches as insufficient because simulators can never capture all real-world edge cases like bumps, gravel, or banana peels. The path forward requires robots that learn live on hardware, similar to how Berkeley got a robot dog walking in 20 minutes of real-world training. He explores the human reward function as a multi-part meta-system encoded in DNA, with components like autoregression, curiosity, and behavioral cloning that must be captured for true AGI.
quotes.key_insight: True AGI requires on-device learning in the real world because no simulator can capture the infinite edge cases of physical reality, and the human reward function itself is too complex to fully specify in advance.

quotes.quote: I define AGI as a machine capable of taking (almost) any human job.

quotes.quote: Here's a simple way to take over Twitter for way less than $43 billion dollars.
quotes.summary: Hotz proposes a crypto-based scheme to 'vampire attack' Twitter by cloning it on a blockchain and incentivizing both content creators and consumers to migrate through token rewards. The plan involves scraping Twitter via browser extensions, rewarding original posters with cryptocurrency, and creating a clean ad-free interface to lure users away. He argues this approach solves the chicken-and-egg problem of platform migration by paying both sides to switch. The post dismisses doubters and claims the technical challenges are solvable through TLS verification and leveraging Twitter's existing anti-Sybil measures.
quotes.key_insight: Platform lock-in can be broken by using cryptocurrency incentives to simultaneously pay both content creators and consumers to migrate, solving the coordination problem that kills most Twitter alternatives.

quotes.quote: The masks really had nothing to do with COVID, they were like wearing a cross around your neck.
quotes.summary: Hotz uses the cloth mask debate as a lens to examine what he sees as a broader societal failure: the inability to admit mistakes and the triumph of performative signaling over truth. He argues that masks became religious symbols rather than medical interventions, and that the lack of accountability when this became clear reveals deep dysfunction. The post connects this to Moloch-style coordination failures, declining trust in institutions, and the atomization of modern life. He concludes pessimistically that 'exit' (opting out of mainstream society) may be the only viable path forward, while calling for new leaders who can articulate a positive vision centered on beauty and truth.
quotes.key_insight: The cloth mask episode revealed not a public health failure but a civilizational one: a society that cannot admit error, prioritizes tribal signaling over truth, and may only be salvageable through exit rather than reform.

quotes.quote: Humanity is not past these things. It's unclear if they ever will be.

quotes.quote: That's only 162 GFLOPS…to be fair, they are sparse.
quotes.summary: Hotz challenges the widely cited estimate that the human brain performs 20 PFLOPS of computation. By working from first principles—calculating energy per neuron firing, total firing events given the brain's 20W power draw, and FLOPS per neuron based on synaptic fan-out—he arrives at only 162 GFLOPS, or at most 16 TFLOPS even accounting for sparsity. This suggests current high-end GPUs may already match human brain compute capacity.
quotes.key_insight: First-principles energy calculations suggest the human brain's compute power is vastly overestimated, and current GPUs may already match it.

quotes.quote: But a far cry from 20 PFLOPS, the classic estimate of brain compute.

quotes.quote: The problem with the state is that is it bad, not that the wrong people are in it.
quotes.summary: Hotz argues that the fundamental problem with the state is not who controls it, but that coercive power over others exists at all. He contends that representative democracy tricks people into supporting state power by making them fear what the opposing side might do if they gain control. Rather than political voice or reform, he advocates for exit—using technology to make state functions obsolete by decentralizing basic needs like healthcare, transportation, and energy. The outcome depends on whether defensive or offensive technology prevails, with cryptography offering hope that defense can win.
quotes.key_insight: The path to freedom isn't reforming or capturing state power, but making it irrelevant through decentralized technology that renders centralized control unnecessary.

quotes.quote: You no longer believe that things will get better, but you are forced to be involved because you fear things will get worse.

quotes.quote: I don't want to live in a world where P = NP.

He contends that representative democracy tricks people into supporting state power by making them fear what the opposing side might do if they gain control. Rather than political voice or reform, he advocates for exit—using technology to make state functions obsolete by decentralizing basic needs like healthcare, transportation, and energy. The outcome depends on whether defensive or offensive technology prevails, with cryptography offering hope that defense can win.
quotes.key_insight: The path to freedom isn't reforming or capturing state power, but making it irrelevant through decentralized technology that renders centralized control unnecessary.

quotes.quote: You find these people around the internet, and they believe that by consuming, they are doing good. That they are fulfilling their role in society. They identify as consumer.
quotes.summary: Hotz argues against the cultural phenomenon of people deriving identity and moral satisfaction from consumption. He traces this to advertising, particularly campaigns like 'The Pepsi Generation' that encouraged brand loyalty as identity. He uses a zoo tiger metaphor to illustrate how consumers have been trained to expect products delivered to them rather than creating or earning things themselves. His solution is radical: abolish advertising and remove the systems that infantilize consumers.
quotes.key_insight: Consumer identity is a manufactured cage that keeps people passive and entitled, and the only way to restore human agency is to abolish the advertising systems that created it.

quotes.quote: At some point in history, we stopped being citizens of a free society and became something for the ruling class to manage.
quotes.summary: Hotz argues that the real power structure in America is an interconnected oligarchy spanning government, corporations, media, NGOs, and intelligence agencies—what some call the 'deep state' but which he describes as simply the same people rotating through a 'revolving door.' He contends that journalism has been fully captured by this regime and now functions as propaganda. Rather than violent revolution, he proposes creating a rigorous, fact-based documentation project to expose the actual structure of power, believing that honest transparency would undermine the regime's ability to maintain control through obfuscation.
quotes.key_insight: The modern power structure maintains control not through visible force but through obscuring its own nature, making rigorous documentation of who actually holds power a more revolutionary act than any protest.

quotes.quote: They don't make chips, they make shares.
quotes.summary: Hotz corrects his previous skepticism about AI chip startups and VLIW architectures. He explains that neural networks are fundamentally DSPs where all loads and stores can be statically computed, unlike CPUs or GPUs. This makes specialized architectures and fancy compilers actually good ideas for deep learning. He acknowledges that AI chip startups fail not due to bad technical tradeoffs but because of economic dysfunction - 'they make shares, not chips.' He concedes that Google's TPU and even Cerebras got the tradeoffs mostly right.
quotes.key_insight: Neural networks are fundamentally different from general-purpose compute - they're DSPs where everything is known except the data - which validates specialized chip architectures that Hotz previously dismissed.

quotes.quote: This is not a counter elite! This is a spin off of the exact same bs that's everywhere.
quotes.summary: George Hotz critiques the newly announced University of Austin, arguing that despite its marketing as a bastion of free thought against mainstream academia, it's actually a spin-off of the existing elite rather than a genuine counter-movement. He points out that its founders (Lonsdale, Altman, Andreessen) are successful products of the current system, and its faculty are merely 'lightly cancelled' establishment figures. Hotz argues that true disruption of universities requires building something 10x better with a positive vision, not just opposing the existing system. He concludes that any serious alternative university project would need to build an accompanying 'religion' or compelling narrative to succeed.
quotes.key_insight: Genuine institutional disruption requires a positive vision and compelling narrative for the future, not establishment figures rebranding their opposition to current trends as revolutionary change.

quotes.quote: We will know we understand life once we can build life. We will know we understand a brain once we can build a brain.
quotes.tone: technical, provocative, dismissive of philosophy, confident
quotes.summary: Hotz presents his framework for building artificial general intelligence based on MuZero's architecture: representation, dynamics, and prediction functions. He argues that machine learning provides better intuition for understanding the brain than neuroscience, and that we'll truly understand life only when we can build it. He details comma.ai's specific implementation using EfficientNet for vision, GRU for temporal modeling, and explains why pure reinforcement learning doesn't work for real-world driving due to incomplete simulators and missing reward functions. The post concludes that driving is the ideal applied AI problem because solving it correctly creates reusable components for harder challenges like cleaning and eventually artificial life.
quotes.key_insight: The path to artificial general intelligence runs through solving driving correctly, because the architecture that works for autonomous vehicles—learned representation, dynamics, and prediction—is the same architecture that will eventually produce artificial life.

quotes.key_insight: The path to artificial general intelligence runs through solving driving correctly, because the architecture that works for autonomous vehicles—learned representation, dynamics, and prediction—is the same architecture that will eventually produce artificial life.

quotes.quote: Software has this beautiful way of being tool complete and much harder to regress than physical things. You can always revert software.
quotes.summary: Hotz discusses his purchase of 123 acres in California with the goal of building an alternative to the university system, but discovers that managing physical infrastructure is fundamentally harder than software because of entropy. Unlike software which can be reverted and automated with tools, physical systems require constant maintenance that doesn't get easier over time. He argues that the professional managerial class derives its power from managing these entropy-fighting human systems. The solution, he believes, is AI and robotics - robotic plumbers and robot-fixers that can maintain themselves with only energy and raw materials as inputs. He's shelving the land project for now because it can't sustain itself without his labor, but sees comma.ai's self-driving work as the first step toward general-purpose robots that will eventually make such projects possible.
quotes.key_insight: The fundamental advantage of software over physical systems is its immunity to entropy, and the path to sustainable physical infrastructure requires closing the automation loop with self-repairing robots.

quotes.quote: Do not leave a power vacuum, the worst people will fill it. Dictatorship is fine as long as there's free exit.
quotes.summary: Hotz argues that a 'professional managerial class' (PMC) dominates modern organizations and society, playing zero-sum political games rather than creating real value. He uses an overheard coffee shop conversation as an exemplar of this mentality—someone focused entirely on internal positioning rather than productive contribution. He contrasts this with comma.ai's culture of dictatorial clarity focused on solving real problems. Hotz believes this parasitic class may comprise 37-73% of society and calls for people to quit or fire PMC members while embracing a growth mindset. He sees crypto and AI as potential tools to defeat this class and restore genuine value creation.
quotes.key_insight: Modern organizations are dominated by a professional managerial class playing zero-sum political games, and the solution is to reject their mentality, focus on genuine value creation, and never leave power vacuums for them to fill.

quotes.quote: I do not respect the New York Times. They have no status or power to convey to me.
quotes.summary: Hotz refuses to speak with the New York Times, citing a complete loss of respect for legacy media institutions. He traces his shift in attitude from respect (pre-2012) to indifference and even contempt, arguing that the institutions once considered powerful no longer hold sway over the people he respects. He expresses optimism about decentralized alternatives—crypto, diverse internet communities, and independent thinkers—as the foundations of a new, more competent order that will eventually emerge.
quotes.key_insight: The old institutions—media, VC, government—have lost their legitimacy among the people actually building the future, and a new decentralized order is quietly emerging to replace them.

quotes.quote: I'm looking for a group house with 6+ people where the general level of technical competence is high.
quotes.summary: George Hotz puts out a brief call to his blog readers asking if anyone knows of technical coliving spaces. He's looking for a group house with six or more residents who have high technical competence. The post serves as a test to see if anyone actually reads his blog, doubling as a practical request. It's a minimalist post that reveals his interest in community living with technically skilled people.
quotes.key_insight: Hotz values surrounding himself with technically skilled people and is interested in communal living arrangements that facilitate this.

quotes.quote: Somebody has to build the things. Somebody has to cook the food.
quotes.summary: Hotz critiques the modern trend toward consumption-focused lifestyles, arguing that the dream of perpetual travel and digital nomadism is unsustainable because someone must create the things being consumed. He sees this as a tragedy of the commons—as more people consume without creating, there's less value in the system. He draws a line between childhood (consumption) and adulthood (creation), and attacks entire industries like advertising and finance as parasitic. The post concludes that when creation fades and only middlemen remain, money itself becomes worthless.
quotes.key_insight: A society that incentivizes consumption over creation through free money and parasitic industries is cannibalizing itself into worthlessness.

quotes.quote: You can't swim upstream forever, entropy always pushes back.
quotes.summary: Hotz argues that meaningful political and social change requires technological disruption rather than cultural or institutional reform. He observes that traditional approaches to changing society—building better institutions or churches—inevitably fall to 'bureaucratic capture' and entropy. The real goal should be enabling true individual sovereignty through technology that eliminates recurring expenses and dependencies on centralized systems. He frames this as a harder problem than self-driving cars but potentially achievable within decades, noting that the technical challenges are secondary to ensuring the technology serves liberation rather than existing power structures.
quotes.key_insight: True individual sovereignty—freedom from dependence on centralized systems—is not a political problem but a technology problem that requires building AI capable of fighting entropy and eliminating recurring expenses.

quotes.quote: Some people make babby. I make computer.
quotes.summary: In this extremely brief post, Hotz draws a stark contrast between conventional life paths (having children) and his own chosen path (building technology). The post distills his entire philosophy of prioritizing creation and innovation over traditional milestones into just six words. It's a declaration of identity and values, suggesting that his legacy will be his technological contributions rather than biological offspring.
quotes.key_insight: Your legacy is defined by what you create, whether that's children or computers—Hotz has made his choice.

quotes.quote: Capitalism is so rigged it isn't fun anymore.
quotes.summary: Hotz argues that capitalism has become so rigged by elites that it's no longer enjoyable, comparing it to cheating at Monopoly. He criticizes hedge funds buying up housing, predatory advertising, and worthless education loans as scams that exploit ordinary people. Despite being smart enough to avoid these scams personally, he fears the inevitable backlash when people 'flip the board over.' He proposes radical policy fixes including a 500% advertising tax, a 1% transaction tax, and defunding universities, but admits the real problem is cultural and the only solution is shame.
quotes.key_insight: The elites rigging capitalism don't see themselves as scammers because they exist in self-congratulatory bubbles, and the only real corrective is social shame—not policy.

quotes.quote: Unless it's a from company with a track record like Apple, secrecy is just cover for 'we don't have shit'
quotes.summary: Hotz provides a critical analysis of the AI chip startup landscape, arguing that most companies are wildly overcapitalized with no actual products to sell, while NVIDIA maintains dominance despite overpricing. He systematically dismantles the technical approaches of major players like Graphcore, Groq, Cerebras, and Google TPU, identifying recurring architectural mistakes like VLIW and complex multicore designs that push too much burden to compilers. The post concludes with a detailed proposal for 'Cherry Computer,' his own AI chip company that would use a simple RISC-V core with wide decode paths and a 32x32x32 matrix multiply unit. He argues this approach would be 10x faster and 10x cheaper than NVIDIA because it would be easy to program in hardware rather than requiring complex software optimization.
quotes.key_insight: AI chip startups keep repeating the same architectural mistakes by pushing complexity to software and compilers, when the winning strategy is to build simple hardware that's easy to program.

quotes.quote: I was not socialized by society. I did not graduate college, I did not do well in school. I am not a part of the existing power structure, and I don't have to play by their rules.
quotes.summary: Hotz argues that current societal elites and power structures are fundamentally broken, unsustainable, and built on fake money and mass indoctrination. Using his 2020 rap lyrics as a framework, he critiques the hollowness of modern institutions, from education to politics, arguing they prioritize control over people rather than genuine progress. He sees technology and the internet as forces that will eventually expose and replace these corrupt systems. The piece ends with hope that a new generation of truth-seekers will rise to build something better, fighting for power over nature rather than power over people.
quotes.key_insight: Modern institutions are hollow facades maintained by fake money and mass indoctrination, but technology and a rising generation of outsiders will eventually replace control over people with mastery over nature.

quotes.quote: I always thought idiots were going to kill me, but I imagined it would be through inaction instead of action.
quotes.summary: Hotz expresses frustration that human incompetence and misaligned priorities pose existential risks. He argues that gain-of-function research on coronaviruses was permitted despite obvious dangers, while potentially beneficial research like human cloning is forbidden. Drawing on his early COVID research, he suggests the virus was a lab escape and laments that with proper resource allocation, immortality could be achievable, but instead humanity stumbles toward self-destruction through both negligence and misguided action.
quotes.key_insight: Society's risk tolerance is bizarrely calibrated—we permit research that kills millions while banning research that might save us, and this inconsistency reflects a deeper incompetence that threatens human survival.

quotes.quote: The smallest animal, even the smallest cell, is capable of survival and reproduction. To date, no technological system is.
quotes.summary: Hotz argues that no technology today can survive without human maintenance, unlike even the simplest biological life. He proposes that truly solving AI means creating machines capable of self-sufficient survival, maintenance, and reproduction. Using his 123-acre land purchase as both inspiration and testing ground, he envisions a future 'silicon ecology' where machines can fight entropy, maintain themselves, and form an ecosystem. He sees comma.ai's self-driving technology as foundational infrastructure for this broader vision of autonomous machine life.
quotes.key_insight: Solving AI isn't about passing benchmarks or matching human performance on tasks—it's about creating technology that can survive and thrive independently, fighting entropy the way biology does.

quotes.quote: I wish tattoos still made you unemployable. Then I'd get some.
quotes.summary: Hotz laments the death of authentic counterculture in technology, arguing that the rebellious spirit of punk rock and dropout entrepreneurs has been completely co-opted by the system. He contends that modern tech startups following the VC playbook are merely 'underpaid research divisions of big tech companies' rather than genuine innovators. The post questions whether meaningful resistance to corporate conformity is even possible anymore, comparing the situation to a decentralized Truman Show where everyone participates in their own oppression. His only hope lies in the possibility that centralized, bureaucratic tech companies can only produce failures like the 737 MAX, while genuine innovation might still come from kids building things in garages just because they can.
quotes.key_insight: True technological rebellion has been co-opted into a conformist VC-funded pipeline, and the only hope for authentic innovation lies in people building things purely for the joy of creation rather than for money or status.

His only hope lies in the possibility that centralized, bureaucratic tech companies can only produce failures like the 737 MAX, while genuine innovation might still come from kids building things in garages just because they can.
quotes.key_insight: True technological rebellion has been co-opted into a conformist VC-funded pipeline, and the only hope for authentic innovation lies in people building things purely for the joy of creation rather than for money or status.

quotes.quote: Put it out of your mind that the AI Control Problem is a mathematical problem with a mathematical solution.
quotes.summary: Hotz argues that the AI control problem is fundamentally a political problem about power, not a mathematical one that can be solved through rationalist frameworks. He criticizes both the rationalist community and modern society as too weak and fractured to handle the unprecedented power that AI will bring. Drawing parallels to historical struggles for dominance, he suggests humanity needs strong leadership—a 'Caesar'—to navigate the coming conflict with AI. He dismisses contemporary politics, social movements, and incremental progress as irrelevant distractions in the face of this existential challenge.
quotes.key_insight: The AI control problem is not a technical puzzle to be solved by smart people, but a civilizational test of whether humanity has the strength and coherence to wield—or resist—unprecedented power.

quotes.quote: Rationality is a wonderful tool, but it is only a tool. It can only deal in is, not in ought.

quotes.quote: Genies do not go back in boxes.
quotes.summary: Hotz critiques the Unabomber's anti-technology stance while agreeing that industrial-technological society is problematic. He proposes a new axis for evaluating technology: does it centralize or decentralize power? Using comma.ai vs Waymo as examples, he argues we should build self-contained, decentralized technology that doesn't require industrial systems or societal cooperation to function. He holds up biology as the ideal model—efficient, beautiful, non-toxic, and decentralized—while condemning companies like Twitter and Facebook as 'industry without technology.'
quotes.key_insight: The crucial question for any technology isn't how advanced it is, but whether it centralizes power into the hands of few or distributes it among many.

quotes.quote: A neuron is an accumulator, and a synapse is a multiplier. Multiply-accumulate. Sound familiar?
quotes.summary: George Hotz breaks down what it would take to build a silicon equivalent of a human brain using current GPU technology. He estimates the brain has 100 trillion synapses operating at 20 PFLOPS, which would require approximately 40,000 A100 GPUs to simulate. The total cost comes to about $20 million in hardware plus $1,600 per hour in electricity. The analysis treats neurons as simple multiply-accumulate operations, arguing that while brains exhibit complex behavior, the computational substrate is straightforward to understand.
quotes.key_insight: Building a silicon equivalent of a human brain is achievable with current technology for around $20 million in hardware, with the main constraint being memory bandwidth rather than raw compute power.

quotes.quote: An economy made up of only these people wouldn't work.
quotes.summary: Hotz argues that all jobs fall into three categories: service jobs that merely circulate existing money (constant), production jobs that create value linearly (farmers, miners, manufacturers), and efficiency-improving jobs that compound exponentially (scientists, engineers). Using a mining town metaphor, he illustrates how economies dependent solely on service jobs inevitably go broke, as they only pass money around while losing value to transaction fees and imports. His analysis of US Bureau of Labor Statistics data reveals that 73% of American jobs are now in the non-value-creating service category. This trend concerns him because a global economy composed primarily of service jobs would collapse like a ghost town.
quotes.key_insight: An economy cannot sustain itself on service jobs alone—only production and innovation create real wealth, yet 73% of US jobs now merely circulate existing money rather than generating new value.
